items.q: Is cached data always safe to use?
items.correct: No - cached data can become stale when the original source changes, so caching strategies must decide when to refresh or invalidate entries.
items.misconception: Yes - once data is cached it is correct forever.
items.random_wrong: Yes - caches update themselves instantly whenever anything changes.
items.why: The speed win hides the freshness cost. Cache invalidation is famously hard precisely because the copy and the source can silently diverge.

items.q: Does running tasks concurrently always make a program faster?
items.correct: No - tasks that depend on each other cannot overlap, and coordination overhead can outweigh gains; only independent work parallelises well.
items.misconception: Yes - every program doubles in speed on two cores.
items.random_wrong: Yes - concurrency removes the need for efficient algorithms.
items.why: Parallel hardware suggests automatic speed-up. Dependencies force waiting, and managing shared data adds cost - sometimes a sequential version wins.

items.q: Does abstraction make a solution less correct?
items.correct: No - abstraction removes only irrelevant detail; the retained model must stay accurate for the task, like a tube map that is exact about connections.
items.misconception: Yes - leaving out details always introduces errors.
items.random_wrong: Yes - abstract solutions cannot be implemented as real programs.
items.why: Students equate detail with truth. The skill is choosing what matters: a good abstraction is fully reliable for its purpose despite saying less.

items.correct: A condition that must hold before a subroutine runs for it to behave correctly
items.misconception: An error message shown after a crash
items.random_wrong: The last line of every program
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A condition that must hold before a subroutine runs for it to behave correctly. “An error message shown after a crash” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Storing previously fetched or computed data for fast reuse
items.why: Correct answer: Storing previously fetched or computed data for fast reuse. “Deleting all temporary files” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Identifying parts of a problem that can be carried out simultaneously
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Identifying parts of a problem that can be carried out simultaneously. “Writing the program twice” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Representing the essential features of a problem while hiding irrelevant detail
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Representing the essential features of a problem while hiding irrelevant detail. “Making the problem deliberately vague” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Two independent tasks each take 4 seconds. Run perfectly in parallel on two processors, how long do they take in total?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 s. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
